The RPO graph is the cleanest answer to which enterprise tech companies are actually winning the AI transition.

In a world where the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative is repricing every company tied to human workflows, RPO growth is the clearest signal separating the survivors from the casualties.

• $PLTR trading at ~84x RPO growing 143%
• $NET trading at ~30x RPO growing 48%
• $MDB trading at ~13x RPO growing 97%
• $CRWD trading at ~12x RPO growing 38%
• $DDOG trading at ~12x RPO growing 52%
• $PANW trading at ~8x RPO growing 23%
• $SNOW trading at ~6x RPO growing 42%
• $TEAM trading at ~5x RPO growing 41%
• $NOW trading at ~4x RPO growing 26%
• $ZS trading at ~4x RPO growing 31%

BIG TECH ESTIMATED 2026 PROFITS

1. $NVDA ~$237B

Andrej Karpathy released a project scoring all 342 U.S. occupations on how exposed they are to AI using an LLM & BLS data.

The college premium that defined American aspiration for decades may get repriced as AI starts doing knowledge work for free & a skilled plumber can out-earn a data analyst.

AI adoption is currently constrained by power, compute & data center capacity.

Because training + inference are extremely energy-intensive, the markets are rewarding the companies building the infrastructure that makes AI compute possible.

Elon Musk says AI could make jobs “optional” in the future.

He believes advanced AI may enable a “universal high income” society.

$PLTR CEO Alex Karp says “You may hate this but the one person protecting your right to be a conspiracy theorist that has a seat at the table is me.”

He argues Palantir’s architecture is built to protect civil liberties while enabling national security operations.

$GOOGL just embedded AI agents into Google Maps turning the world’s largest navigation app into a conversational discovery engine for the local economy.

Instead of searching categories, users ask questions & Google’s decides which businesses get surfaced to billions of users.

PETER LYNCH’S FAVORITE METRIC IS THE PEG RATIO

PEG < 1 usually means mispriced growth
PEG > 2 starts to push into the danger zone

Popular names right now:
• $TSLA ~3.5x
• $AAPL ~2.9x
• $PLTR ~2.8x
• $GOOGL ~1.7x
• $HOOD ~1.6x
• $TSM ~1.6x
• $ASML ~1.6x
• $NOW ~1.3x
• $MSFT ~1.2x
• $META ~1.2x
• $AMZN ~1.2x
• $ALAB ~1.1x
• $SOFI ~1.0x
• $CRM ~0.9x
• $ADBE ~0.9x
• $NVDA ~0.9x
• $ORCL ~0.6x
• $AVGO ~0.5x
• $HIMS ~0.5x
• $AMD ~0.4x
• $MU ~0.4x

Peter Lynch favored the PEG ratio because it ties valuation to growth.

I prefer using it over a 2026–2028 window so valuation adjusts for how fast the business is actually compounding.

For example, $GOOGL may trade at ~26x forward earnings but EPS is expected to grow from $11.51 in 2026 to $15.31 in 2028 implying ~15% earnings CAGR so their PEG is ~1.7x.

Anduril landing a $20B government contract shows how defense budgets are starting to flow into the new-age defense names like $PLTR, $ONDS, $KRKNF, $RKLB & $IONQ. 

Legacy defense moved slow & billed more but this new generation gets paid to move fast, ship real tech & iterate.

$IREN is expected to generate over $1B in operating profits by 2028 from the 50K $NVDA B300 GPUs on order bringing its total fleet to 150K.

The company controls ~4.5 GW of secured power capacity while needing only ~500 MW to support its $3.7B ARR target by year end.

That is roughly a 10:1 power advantage few neocloud players can replicate quickly.

$MELI CEO says fintech is the company’s biggest opportunity ahead.

MercadoPago is growing ~50% YoY with a long runway as financial services penetration across most of Latin America remains very low.

The Evolution of Money

This is the Rubymar. It sank in March 2024 in the Bab el-Mandeb after the Houthis said they were targeting only U.S., U.K., and Israeli-linked ships.

* Belize flag
* Marshall Islands owner, with a UK address on its papers
* Lebanese management
* Unclear insurance
* Saudi commercial link
* Bound for Bulgaria with fertilizer cargo

This is the problem with the “test” Iran is now proposing: what exactly is a U.S., Israeli, or allied ship?

Do they mean:

* the country whose flag it flies?
* the country of ownership?
* the country of management?
* the country of insurance?
* the country of commercial links?
* the origin or destination of the cargo?

Once you run down that list, a huge share of global shipping has some U.S., U.K., or Israeli lineage somewhere in the chain.

Russian, Iranian, and Chinese shipping may pass that test more easily. But those ships were already moving this week anyway.

So, what has really changed here? Not the risk. Just the branding.

Remember that just yesterday, Tehran was reportedly floating a completely different condition: cargo paid in yuan. Today, it’s “any country except the U.S. and Israel can pass.” That is not a reopening. It is evidence the rules are being improvised in real time.

And two days ago, on March 12, Iran tried to pose as reasonable by saying ships must coordinate with its navy to transit. Reuters also reported other selective assurances for passage, including a vessel changing its signaling to “China-owner” to get through.

We’ve seen this before. During the Red Sea crisis in 2024, the Houthis offered similarly selective assurances in Bab el-Mandeb. In practice, it was confusing, chaotic, and did little to restore the normal flow of ships. Reuters reported that many vessels caught up in those attacks had no Israeli connection, and UNCTAD said Suez Canal transits fell 42% from peak levels.

Shipping does not normalize because of slogans. It normalizes when passage is clear, verifiable, insurable, and durable. Right now, it is none of those things.

So this is not Iran loosening its grip on the Strait of Hormuz. It’s PR spin designed to sound reasonable.

Until owners, crews, charterers, and insurers can move without ad hoc political tests or special permission, the Strait remains squeezed.
Desperately seeking a pardon
